queries: What is the history of the company Apple?

items.query: What is the history of the company Apple?

results.label: Tavily Search Advanced + Answer
results.output:
# Tavily Search Advanced Answer

Apple was founded on April 1 1976 in Los Angeles by Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, and Ronald Wayne, initially operating out of Jobs’s garage and financing the venture by selling personal items; the company’s first product, the Apple I, debuted that summer and was followed by the mass‑market Apple II in 1977, which helped establish Apple as a leading personal‑computer maker. After incorporating in 1977 and receiving seed funding from Mike Markkula, Apple went public on December 12 1980 at $22 a share, creating hundreds of millionaires on its first day. The Macintosh, introduced in 1984, brought the graphical user interface to a wider audience, but internal power struggles led to Jobs’s departure in 1985; the company struggled through the 1990s until Jobs returned as interim CEO in 1997, reviving the brand with the iMac in 1998 and launching the iPod in 2001, the iTunes Store in 2003, and the revolutionary iPhone in 2007, which was quickly followed by the App Store (2008) and iPad (2010). After Jobs’s resignation due to health issues in 2011, Tim Cook became CEO, overseeing the expansion into wearables with the Apple Watch (2015) and AirPods (2016), guiding Apple to become the world’s most valuable publicly traded company, first surpassing $1 trillion in market value in 2018 and reaching $2 trillion by the early 2020s; recent milestones include the launch of Apple Vision Pro in 2023 and the celebration of Apple’s 50th anniversary in 2026.

## Sources

On April 12, less than two weeks after the company's formation, Wayne left Apple, selling his 10% share back to the two Steves for $800.( [...] On December 12, 1980, Apple went public on the NASDAQ stock exchange with the ticker symbol "AAPL", selling 4.6 million shares at $22 per share ($.10 per share when adjusting for stock splits as of November 30, 2020( generating over $100 million, which was more capital than any IPO since Ford Motor Company in 1956.( Several venture capitalists cashed out, reaping billions in long-term capital gains. [2] Apple Inc. timeline
URL: https://www.lucensoftware.com/blog/apple-inc-timeline
Snippet: Download free PowerPoint file

Apple Inc. was born on the 1st of April 1976 and was founded by Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, and Ronald Wayne. The company’s first product was the Apple I, a personal computer designed and hand-built entirely by Wozniak.

Gradually expanding its range of products to consumer electronics and online services, it took the pioneering venture less than half a century to become the world’s largest smartphone manufacturer.

## Apple history timeline: key milestones

The Apple Inc. Timeline overviews the important events in the history of the business, including releases of their main products and services. Let’s retrace the major milestones of their journey since establishment until the present day: [...] 1976: Apple Computer Company is founded
 1976: Apple I
 1977: Apple II
 1980: Apple III
 1980: Company goes public for $22 per share
 1984: The Macintosh
 1985: S. Jobs leaves the company
 1989: The Macintosh Portable
 1990: 3 new Macintosh models
 1991: The PowerBook
 1997: S. Jobs regains position as CEO
 1997: Apple Online Store
 1998: The iMac
 2001: First retail stores
 2001: The iPod
 2003: iTune Store
 2007: Launches the iPhone & Apple TV under new name: Apple Inc.
 2008: App Store
 2010: The iPad
 2010: The iPhone 4
 2010: Apple shares reach an all-time high
 2011: Jobs resigns (health problems)
 2012: The iPhone 5
 2017: Clips app
 2015: Apple Watch
 2016: AirPods
 2018: Apple’s value reaches $1 trillion
 2023: Apple Vision Pro
 2026: 50th anniversary [...] ## An in-depth look at the milestones in Apple's history

Let’s take a closer look at how Apple became creator of game-changing devices like the Mac, iPhone and iPod:

#### 1976: Apple Computer Company is founded, and Apple I is born

The identity behind one of the world’s most profitable brands emerged in the garage of Steve Jobs’ childhood home, after he came up with the idea of selling Steve Wozniak’s hand-built computer Apple I. To finance the creation of the device, Jobs sold his VW Kombi van and Wozniak his HP-65 calculator. The prototype was released on the 11th of April 1976, demonstrated 4 months later at the Homebrew Computer Club, and sold at the introductory price of $666 (the equivalent of $2,867 in 2017).

#### 1977: Apple II

[3] Apple Inc. History, Impacts & Facts | Study.com
URL: https://study.com/academy/lesson/apple-inc-history-products-facts-company.html
Snippet: ## History of Apple Computer Inc.

The Apple company began with the dream of Steve Wozniak, who was then an intern at the Hewlett-Packard Company, to build his own compact computer. His dream came true in 1976, when Wozniak built his first microcomputer, however, Hewlett-Packard was not interested in his invention. At that point, Wozniak partnered with his high school friend, Steve Jobs and began production in Job's garage. [...] ### The 2010s: Apple post-Steve Jobs

In 2011, Apple changed the world with its introduction of the iPhone 4S with groundbreaking Siri AI technology. In August of that year, Steve Jobs left Apple due to his declining health, passing away in October. He was replaced as CEO by Tim Cook. Under the leadership of Cook, the company did not introduce new products for several years. Instead, Apple made improvements to its previously released devices, releasing numerous versions of the iPhone and creating two new versions of the iPad: the smaller Mini and the larger Pro. Two new products came to market in 2015: the first iWatch and AirPods. In 2018, Apple became the first company in history to be valued at one trillion dollars. Two years later, it would beat its own record, doubling that amount. [...] To unlock this lesson you must be a Study.com memberCreate an account

## Lesson Summary

Apple Inc. is one of the world's foremost innovators of technology. Inventions such as the Mac personal computer, iPhone, smart technology, and Siri helped to propel the Apple company to the forefront of the technology industry. Apple founders Steve Wozniak and Steve Jobs started the business in Jobs' garage. Today, Apple Inc. is a multinational company valued at nearly four billion dollars and led by Tim Cook. Although Apple Inc. has had many successes, it is not without its controversies. However, without the influence of Apple, the technology market would likely not be as advanced as it is today.

Snippet: Wozniak, then a 26-year-old engineering intern at Hewlett-Packard, presented his design to his employer. When the company expressed no interest, Wozniak teamed up with a former high school classmate, 21-year-old Steve Jobs. The two decided to set up their operations in the Jobs family garage, founding Apple Inc. on April 1, 1976, along with Ronald Wayne, an experienced tech industry executive who would play a role in helping facilitate and integrate the young founders’ company vision and operational pursuits.

Jobs and Wozniak each held a 45% stake in the company; Wayne had a 10% partnership stake. To fund their venture with working capital, Jobs sold his Volkswagen minibus and Wozniak his programmable calculator. [...] With the help of its first angel investor and Intel Corporation veteran Armas Clifford “Mike” Markkula, the company unveiled its inaugural product, the Apple I, in July 1976 at a Homebrew Computer Club meeting. Selling for $666.66, the Apple I was a bare motherboard with a basic CPU, random access memory (RAM), and video capabilities, but no keyboard or monitor. Nevertheless, the company’s first product marked the garage start-up’s first success—however minor—selling 200 units that year. Later that year, Ronald Wayne decided to leave the fledgling company, selling back his 10% share for $800.

## 1977–1978: Apple II and early commercial success

In January 1977, with Markkula providing his expertise and seed funding of $250,000, Apple Inc. was incorporated. [...] Founded by Steve Jobs and Steve Wozniak in 1976, Apple set new benchmarks in product innovation, user-centric functionality, aesthetics and design, and multiproduct integration. Apple redefined and transformed the capabilities of modern computing. Further, Apple innovated the industry by establishing a marketplace ecosystem for third-party application developers, leveraging this new economy to expand its products’ functionalities and strengthen its position. The company is headquartered in Cupertino, California.

# Apple Inc.

Apple, Inc. is an American computer and technology company. Apple has been a leader in consumer electronics innovation for almost fifty years, drawing praise for its aesthetic-oriented product design led by co-founder Steve Jobs and its groundbreaking computing advances led by co-founder Steve Wozniak. 1

### At-A-Glance

1976

Tim Cook

Contents

Since the resignation and death of Jobs in 2011, Apple under CEO Tim Cook has become one of the most dominant technology companies in the world, rivaling Google, Microsoft, and Amazon. As of January 2021, Apple was the second-largest company in the world, with a market capitalization of $2.2 trillion. 1

Jobs, like Wozniak before him, attended Homestead High School in Cupertino, California, a solidly middle-class school in the suburbs of Silicon Valley. They met in 1971 through a mutual friend. In about 1975, the two friends began attending meetings of the Home- brew Computer Club, a group of Silicon Valley electronics hobbyists that met to explore the latest in new computer technology. Wozniak, who had no formal engineering training, designed a new single-board computer called the Apple-1, he says, “as a way of show- ing off to the people at the Homebrew Club.” [...] had created more than 100,000 apps for the device, ranging from navigation aids to cameras to wildly popular games. One of the visions articulated by Jobs was to create an ecosystem in which Apple products could thrive. On August 21, 2012, Apple became the most valuable company in history, with a market capitalization of $620 billion. The company’s story touches upon many aspects of the American dream: its humble origins, the “wilderness years” when Jobs sought to find his next great project (as well as himself), his triumphant return, [...] great project (as well as himself), his triumphant return, and the new frontiers Jobs and Apple explored and then settled. Over its history, Apple has persisted, surviving near-death experiences, changing market conditions, constantly changing technology, and stiff competition. Ultimately, it focused on a strategy of combining high-end consumer devices with groundbreaking software and a built-in e-commerce ecosystem. Despite Jobs no longer being at Apple, the company continues to innovate, highlighting Apple’s world-class technical and design talent accumulated and honed over many years. results.label: Research Mini Lite
results.output:
Apple Inc. was founded on April 1, 1976, by Steve Jobs, Steve Wozniak, and Ronald Wayne in the Jobs family garage. The company initially focused on personal computers, with the Apple I being their first product. Apple revolutionized the technology sector by creating the first commercially successful personal computer and popularizing the graphical user interface (GUI).

Throughout the 1980s, Apple found success with the Macintosh line of computers. However, the company faced decline and restructuring in the 1990s. The return of Steve Jobs in 1997 marked a turnaround, leading to a period of renewed profitability and innovation.

In the 2000s, Apple expanded beyond computers into mobile devices, launching the iPod, iPhone, and iPad, which transformed the consumer electronics market. The company also developed its own software and digital content platforms like iTunes and the App Store, establishing a strong ecosystem.

Apple is known for its emphasis on design, user experience, and integration of hardware and software. It has grown to become the largest company in the world by market capitalization, valued at over $3 trillion as of early 2024.

Key milestones include:
- 1976: Founding and first Apple computer.
- 1980s: Success with Macintosh.
- 1990s: Decline and restructuring.
- 1997: Steve Jobs returns, company turnaround.
- 2000s: Launch of iPod, iPhone, iPad.
- 2018: First company to reach $1 trillion valuation.
- 2024: Market value around $3 trillion.

Apple's history reflects its evolution from a garage startup to a global technology leader known for innovation in hardware, software, and digital services [2][3][5][1].

References:
1. https://finance.yahoo.com/news/history-apple-company-stock-142145068.html
2. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Apple_Inc.
3. https://www.britannica.com/money/Apple-Inc
5. https://www.ebsco.com/research-starters/computer-science/apple-inc

results.label: Tavily Research Mini
results.output:
# Apple Inc.: A Chronological History

## 1. Founding and Early Days (1976‑1980)

Apple Computer Company was founded on **April 1 1976** by **Steve Jobs**, **Steve Wozniak**, and **Ronald Wayne** in the garage of Jobs’s parents in Los Altos, California (.). Wayne, who had previously worked at Atari, received a 10 % stake but sold it back to Jobs and Wozniak for **$800** just twelve days after incorporation (.).  

The fledgling firm’s first product, the **Apple I**, was a hand‑built motherboard sold for **$666.66**; 200 units were sold in 1976, providing the modest cash flow needed to survive [1]. The venture was funded by **Mike Markkula**, an early angel investor who contributed **$250,000** in seed capital and brought essential business expertise [1].  

In **January 1977** Apple was formally incorporated in Cupertino, California, and the **Apple II** launched later that year, becoming the first mass‑market personal computer with color graphics and an integrated keyboard . Revenue exploded, growing from **$775,000** in 1977 to **$118 million** by 1980, an average annual growth rate of **533 %** .

## 2. The Macintosh Era and Early Leadership (1981‑1996)

The **Macintosh project** began in September 1979 under Jeff Raskin, who envisioned a low‑cost portable computer. Steve Jobs, reassigned from the Lisa project in January 1981, seized control, refocusing the Mac as a high‑performance desktop with a mouse‑driven graphical user interface powered by the Motorola 68000 processor (.). The iconic **Macintosh** debuted in 1984 with a celebrated Super Bowl commercial directed by Ridley Scott, positioning Apple as a cultural counter‑hero to IBM [1].  

Leadership in the early 1980s was marked by **Michael Scott** (1977‑1981), **Mike Markkula** (1981‑1983), and **John Sculley** (1983‑1993). Under Sculley, Apple introduced the **Mac Portable** (1989) and the **PowerBook** line (1991). A strategic pivot came in **1991** when Apple formed the **AIM alliance** with IBM and Motorola, launching the **Power Macintosh** line based on PowerPC RISC processors (.). The alliance aimed to create a new computing platform, though it ultimately fell short of displacing Microsoft’s dominance (.).

## 3. Crisis, Return of Jobs, and the “Think Different” Renaissance (1997‑2007)

Apple’s market share eroded through the early‑1990s, prompting a board‑room coup that installed **Gilbert Amelio** as CEO (1996‑1997). In **1997**, Apple acquired **NeXT** for **$400 million**, bringing Steve Jobs back as an advisor and soon as interim CEO [2]. Jobs streamlined the product line, canceling 70 % of models, and forged a crucial partnership with Microsoft, securing a **$150 million investment** and continued Office support [3].  

The **1998 iMac** marked a design breakthrough, emphasizing translucent aesthetics and ease of use [4]. This design‑first philosophy, cultivated under Jobs, became a core cultural trait, fostering a reputation for minimalist, user‑centric products [5]. The **iPod** (2001) and the **iTunes Store** (2003) extended Apple’s ecosystem into digital media, while the **2007 launch of the iPhone** and **Apple TV** rebranded the company as **Apple Inc.**, signaling a shift from computers to consumer electronics [6].  

During this period Apple’s financial profile transformed: by the early 2000s the company posted its first **$1 billion** annual retail sales, and the iPhone’s success propelled revenue beyond **$100 billion** by 2015 [7].

## 4. The Tim Cook Era: Services, Wearables, and Market‑Cap Milestones (2011‑2025)

When **Tim Cook** succeeded Jobs in **August 2011**, Apple entered a phase of operational scaling and diversification [3]. Cook emphasized supply‑chain efficiency, renewable energy, and privacy, steering Apple toward a **services‑centric** model that now contributes over **30 %** of total revenue [8]. Key product introductions included the **Apple Watch** (2015), **AirPods** (2016), and the **Apple Vision Pro** (2023).  

Financially, Apple became the world’s first **$1 trillion** market‑cap company in **August 2018**, doubled to **$2 trillion** in **2020**, and briefly reached **$3 trillion** in early **2022** [9]. By **2025**, market capitalization hovered around **$4 trillion**, reflecting sustained earnings growth, aggressive share buybacks, and a thriving services ecosystem [10].

## 5. Leadership Transition to John Ternus (2026‑Present)

In **April 2026**, Apple announced that Tim Cook would step down as CEO to become **Executive Chairman**, appointing **John Ternus**, senior vice‑president of hardware engineering, as the new CEO effective **September 1 2026** [11]. The board highlighted Ternus’s deep technical knowledge and relentless focus on product excellence as essential for the company’s “extraordinary future” [11]. Industry commentary notes that this transition is being managed with the same meticulous succession planning that has characterized Apple’s leadership changes for decades [12].

## 6. Strategic Pivots and Ecosystem Expansion

Apple’s strategic pivots have consistently aligned with its core business model of **integrated hardware‑software‑services**. The shift from **PowerPC** to Intel processors in 2006, and later to its own **Apple Silicon** (M1, M2) in 2020, illustrates a pattern of leveraging internal capabilities to control performance and cost [8].  

The **services pivot**—including **Apple Music**, **iCloud**, **App Store**, and **Apple Pay**—was a deliberate response to the maturing smartphone market, diversifying revenue and deepening user lock‑in [8]. More recently, Apple has begun **democratizing its services** by extending Apple Music to non‑Apple platforms and enabling AirPlay on third‑party TVs, signaling a willingness to broaden its ecosystem beyond its own hardware [13].

## 7. Cultural Impact and Design Legacy

Apple’s cultural imprint stems from a **design‑first philosophy** that prizes simplicity, elegance, and seamless integration [14]. This ethos, rooted in Steve Jobs’s insistence on “design excellence,” has cultivated an **unparalleled brand loyalty** where customers often identify as “Apple enthusiasts” rather than mere consumers [5].  

Organizationally, Apple maintains a **secretive, high‑performance culture** that rewards creativity and rigor, a legacy of Jobs’s practice of firing under‑performers and a continuation under Cook [15]. The company’s commitment to **privacy, sustainability, and accessibility** further reinforces its cultural narrative, positioning Apple as a socially responsible tech leader .

## 8. Current State (Mid‑2026)

As of mid‑2026, Apple operates a **global ecosystem** spanning smartphones, tablets, wearables, personal computers, AR/VR headsets, and a suite of services serving **over 2 billion** active devices. The **Vision Pro** represents Apple’s entry into spatial computing, while the **Apple Silicon** roadmap promises continued performance gains across all product lines. Financially, the company reports **annual revenues exceeding $400 billion**, a **cash reserve surpassing $200 billion**, and a market cap near **$4 trillion** [10].  

Under John Ternus, Apple is expected to double‑down on hardware innovation while expanding services and sustainability initiatives, aiming to “leave the planet better than we found it,” a mantra articulated by Tim Cook during his transition [10]. The company’s future trajectory will likely continue to blend **design excellence**, **ecosystem integration**, and **strategic pivots** that keep it at the forefront of technology and culture.

---

tavily_search_advanced.rationale: Provides a detailed, chronological history covering founding, key products, leadership changes, and recent milestones with citations from reputable sources. The narrative is coherent and well-structured but somewhat summary-like without deep analysis or comparison.

research_mini_lite.rationale: Gives a concise overview touching on major milestones and company evolution with some references. However, it lacks depth, detailed sourcing, and synthesis beyond a straightforward summary.

tavily_research_mini.rationale: Extremely thorough and well-structured report with detailed chronological history, leadership transitions, strategic pivots, cultural impact, and current state. Uses numerous authoritative sources with citations. Provides synthesis by explaining implications and contextualizing events. Clear, well-organized, and easy to scan.

quality.summary: All reports cover Apple's founding and key milestones, but tavily_research_mini stands out for its depth, comprehensive sourcing, and insightful synthesis of Apple's strategic and cultural evolution. Tavily_search_advanced offers a solid, well-sourced summary but less analytical depth, while research_mini_lite provides a brief overview with limited detail and sourcing.
